coredata.dc:title: Photodynamic viral inactivation assisted by photosensitizers

coredata.dc:description: The deadly viruses, which are spreading worldwide at an alarming rate, are a major challenge for the life sciences. More efficient and cost-effective methods with fewer side effects can provide a good alternative to traditional drug-based methods. Currently, physical phenomena such as light in the form of photodynamic action are increasingly being used to inactivate viruses. Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) uses a photosensitizer (PS), light, and oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to inactivate microorganisms. This article reviews the use of existing PSs, as one of the essential anti-viral agents, and introduces new materials and strategies combined with PDI. Physiochemical properties of PSs and their role in interaction with virus components are discussed. Furthermore, the effectiveness of optical sensitizers with radiation methods to inactivate viruses is highlighted.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Combination of Ce6 with other photosensitizers

New generation of photosensitizers and their derivatives for viral PDI
Combination of photosensitizer-mediated PDI with photobiomodulation (PBM)
PDI in deep tissue using up conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Jablonski diagram and mechanism of production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [35], B) and the effect of optical sensitizers on virus photodynamic inactivation (PDI).

The chemical structure of some photosensitizers (PSs) used in photodynamic inactivation (PDI) against cells infected by SARS-CoV-2 virus models. The microscopic images represent the infected cells with no PDI treatment (as untreated control), and the infected cells with PDI mediated by PSs as follows, respectively, curcumin (10 % wt. Cur@PLGA-NPs plus blue laser light, 522.8 J/cm2) [20], phthalocyanine (1 μM, 3.75 J/cm2) [27], titanium oxide (300 μg/mL TiO2 nanoparticle, UV-C light 254 nm, 10 min) [16], Photodithazine [28], and Membrane-Targeting DTTPB (1 μM, 9 mW cm−2 white light for 20 min) [50]. The graphs represent the virucidal assay for measuring the inactivated virus. The schematic illustration of DTTPB demonstrates designed photosensitizer bears a hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails, mimicking the structure of phospholipids on biological membranes. Adapted and reprinted with permission from mentioned references.

The negative electrostatic potential field of the viral envelope has a key role in virion interactions with cationic photosensitizers. (A) The schematic pictures demonstrate the ultrastructural changes of Influenza virion membrane during photodynamic inactivation using cationic phthalocyanine, leading to a “bald” virion [24]. (B) The list of enveloped viruses under PDI treatment by cationic PSs. The comparison of TEM images reveal the PDI mediated-structural changes dependent on the type and concentration of PS as well as the virus strain [24,61,77,82].

Dual application tool of anti-viral photoimmunotherapy not only specifically kill the HIV-infected cells but inactivate the virus. (A, a) Target-specific cell death in response to photoimmunotherapy in HIV Env-expressing 293T cells. (A, b) When PICs bound to HIV Env on the cell membrane, the light irradiation results in rapid necrotic cell death via physical damage to membrane [147]. (B, a) In virus photo-inactivation model, PIC bound to HIV Env on the virion. (B, b) After irradiation, the membrane of virions inactivated with porphyrin or porphyrin-antibody destroyed but their membranes appeared to maintain structural integrity [8]. Adapted and reprinted with permission from Ref. [8]. Copyright © 2017 American Chemical Society.

Schematic images depict the process of PDI by UCNP conjugated with antibody-PSs. This complex nanoparticle has three compartments; antibody for targeting viruses, UCNP for photon or energy source in deep tissue, and porphyrin PS for 1O2 generation. The cells targeted by the complex were irradiated by NIR, activating PSs via either light emission or LRET (A). Consequently, singlet oxygen generation by PS caused cell death (B), leading to the cytotoxicity of HIV Env-transfected cells (C) [153].
Studied different techniques for the photodynamic inactivation of viruses.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The deadly viruses, which are spreading worldwide at an alarming rate, are a major challenge for the life sciences. More efficient and cost-effective methods with fewer side effects can provide a good alternative to traditional drug-based methods. Currently, physical phenomena such as light in the form of photodynamic action are increasingly being used to inactivate viruses. Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) uses a photosensitizer (PS), light, and oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to inactivate microorganisms. This article reviews the use of existing PSs, as one of the essential anti-viral agents, and introduces new materials and strategies combined with PDI. Physiochemical properties of PSs and their role in interaction with virus components are discussed. Furthermore, the effectiveness of optical sensitizers with radiation methods to inactivate viruses is highlighted.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the development of chemical antiviral drugs to control deadly viruses, such as human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and SARS-CoV-2 viruses, has been one of the most important activities of scientists to reduce viral infections [1]. The main functions of drugs are blocking and interrupting the virus's membrane, such as inactivating cellular receptors to inhibit attachment to them, inactivating genetic contents (DNA or RNA) and strengthening the immune system [1,2]. There are significant challenges in the design of vaccines and chemical drugs, including rapid viral mutations, spike protein alterations, and subsequent alterations in viral antigenicity, systemic complementation of blocked target, poor in vitro to in vivo translation, risk of immune deregulation, enhanced cellular toxicities, reduced target specificity [2]. As alternatives and avoiding chemical methods, scientists have proposed new solutions for inactivating viruses by presenting methods based on physical phenomena.

Enveloping viruses are surrounded by an outer lipid membrane that sensitizes them to environmental phenomena such as heat and light, making them easier to inactivate than uncovered viruses [3,4]. For several hundreds of years, light has been recognized as a potential therapeutic device. It has been used mainly in combination with reactive chemicals to treat various diseases such as vitiligo, psoriasis, and different cancers like skin cancer [5]. Hence, applying the irradiation-dependent tools individually or combined with sensitizers and fluorescent materials as a marker is considered efficient in microbial inactivation [3,6].

The use of light, due to its non-invasive nature, could be efficient for many biological purposes. The concept of photodynamic inactivation (PDI) is based on the absorption of light by photosensitizer (PS) molecules in a specific wavelength to be activated and then react with oxygen to generate oxidant species which may cause an eruption in malignant cells or damage the virus [7,8]. PSs contribute to the inactivation of microorganisms and ultimately to the cure of diseases by absorbing light, transferring energy to molecular oxygen, and forming ROS. Various PSs with physicochemical properties such as activation wavelength, stability, chemical polarization, size, biocompatibility, solubility, and photobleaching rate have shown different functions in PDI [3].

As for the clinical application of PDI in viral infections, most studies have been performed on skin regions and small areas. Nowadays, the application of this promising technique in deeper regions is also being considered. Currently, external viral PDI is included in the clinical treatment of skin lesions caused by herpes simplex virus (HSV), human papillomavirus (HPV) [9], varicella zoster virus (VZV) [10], and recently on management of COVID-19 [11]. Although studies of systemic therapy have included far more serious conditions such as recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP), only HPV treatment has been included in internal viral PDI [12].

This paper critically reviews radiation-dependent physical methods based on photosensitizers and studies to inactivate viruses, especially enveloped viruses such as HIV, influenza, or hepatitis, which are responsible for severe problems in humans (Table 1 ).

After thriving the light technology in the field of lasers, LED, portability, etc. the viral photodynamic inactivation (PDI) attracted a great deal of attention [30]. Until nowadays, the majority of PDI methods have been allotted to the treatment of virus-mediated diseases [31]. In 1930, photosensitizers (PSs) were used for targeting and destroying viruses in photodynamic procedures. For the first time, in the early 1970s, the PDI approach was applied to treating herpes infection, particularly for herpes genitals. In vitro evaluations demonstrated that different types of viruses had been effectively inactivated by photodynamic treatment [32].

Generally speaking, the mechanism involves a combined action of three fundamental components which are needed to produce a photodynamic action: i) light with a specific wavelength (depending on the absorption spectrum of PS); ii) molecular oxygen (O2); and iii) a photosensitizing molecule. When a PS is irradiated by a light source with a specific wavelength, it undergoes an electronic transition from a low energy ground state (S0) to an excited singlet state (S1). Then, this PS may decay to S0 state by fluorescence emission or undergo a transition to a triplet state via the intersystem crossing process. In this step, the triplet state photosensitizing molecule (3 PS) can react with O2 via two pathways: i) via type-I mechanism, that PS becomes a radical through electron transfer and/or proton removal, and rapidly reacts with O2 producing reactive oxidative intermediates, e.g., superoxide ion (°O2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (°OH). ii) On the other hand, via type-II mechanism through energy transfer to O2 results in the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2), a highly reactive oxidative species [33] (Fig. 1 A). In either type-I or type-II, the presence of molecular oxygen leads to reactive oxidative species (ROS) generation.

PSs can be categorized based on the type of ROS they generate, however photosensitized reactions are dependent on the molecular oxygen, not the forms of ROS [33]. In addition, the efficacy of activated PSs is dependent on the proximity to the substrates, that make them severely limited to neighboring molecules. Singlet oxygen and ROS can travel up to 75 and 20 nm of interaction radii, respectively [34]. In contrast, the size of enveloped virions, such as HIV, is about 100 nm diameter. Thus, the activated PSs may affect the targeted molecule on the virus, regardless of the ROS forms (Fig. 1B).

Various antiviral therapies against COVID-19 are under investigation, and currently, the FDA authorizes a few antiviral treatments of COVID-19 in certain conditions. Beyond investigational therapies, established intensive care unit protocols typically used to treat acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) are failing in patients with severe COVID-19. Photodynamic methods to inactivate viruses are potential candidates for treating COVID-19 and ARDS, as they have some advantages over pharmaceutical therapies. Some computational studies claim that a photosensitizer-like structure with a large ring-like organic compound, including methylene blue [36] and heme [37], may have specific binding to envelope protein (E), nucleocapsid phosphoprotein (N), and ORF3a of SARS-CoV-2 virus [38], however, the interpretation of computational results has been criticized and needs to be validated [39].

One advantage of PDI is its resistance to virus mutations, as PDI works independently of viral genome. PSs use ROS to damage viral envelope proteins and/or nucleic acids. Therefore, the PDI mechanism is most likely not sensitive to the mutations of SARS-CoV-2, which has been reported, e.g., D614G of the spike protein [40]. ROS-mediated damages to the viral envelope may generate debris or fragments which could stimulate host immune defense. Moreover, viral PDI is affordable compared to other therapeutics like monoclonal antibodies (e.g., Ronapreve), which can be a principal factor for preventive use at home. Other advantages of PDI include high repeatability without viral resistance, fast removal of virus in a noticeably short time with no long-term toxicity, and less damage to adjacent tissues [38].

Photosensitizers such as riboflavin [14,41], Rose Bengal [42,43], curcumin [44,45], methylene blue (MB) [13,46,47], indocyanine green (ICG) [48,49], etc. may potentially generate singlet oxygen and substantially promotes the efficacy of viral inactivation. Generally, it has been proven that light-irradiated PSs can generate ROSs as active phototoxic agents, which can interact with viral nucleic acids, lipids, and proteins and damage the targeted viruses (Fig. 2 ). Modern PDT has its roots in porphyrin-derived agents; hence, by now, most of the clinically approved PSs have tetrapyrrole structure. As an example, porfimer sodium (Photofrin), which has been clinically approved for various cancers PDT, is not a single chemical entity; it is a mixture of oligomers formed by ether and ester linkages of up to eight porphyrin units.

The rhizome of turmeric (Curcuma longa L.) contains a hydrophobic polyphenol called curcumin, a valuable natural PS with anticancer, optical, and pharmacological properties. The biochemical studies determine the manufacturing processes and storage conditions to further apply these drugs for future clinical applications for oral infections. Curcumin has a broadband absorption spectrum with the highest absorption peak at a wavelength of 425 nm, which may be due to the low-energy -* excitation of curcumin [51]. Some of the limitations reported for the potential clinical application of curcumin in PDT are its poor solubility, stability and photostability in aqueous solutions, as well as the rapid metabolism and systemic elimination [44].

Growing evidence has shown that curcumin in the photo-bleaching experiments produces ROS, more specifically singlet state oxygen, hydrogen peroxide, and hydroxyl radicals after the blue light activation between 300 and 500 nm to achieve the above characteristics [52,53]. PDT combined with the usage of curcumin PS could enhance the function of anti-bacterial [54]. Blanco KC et al. reported upper respiratory tract infections (URTI) were caused by acute, chronic, or recurrent infections, including pharyngitis and tonsillitis. The use of curcumin formulations in PDT presented a microbial reduction of 5 logs [45]. Several in vitro studies demonstrated that the use of anti-microbial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) can be effective against Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus species and other bacteria responsible for acute pharyngitis [45,55–57]. These investigations have shown LED irradiation could activate curcumin to increase the percentage of S. aureus bacterial death for preventing infections. The photodynamic action of LED-activated curcumin on cell viability, membrane permeability, and the intracellular ROS of S. aureus was identified [58]. Blue light-activated curcumin markedly damaged membrane permeability, resulting in cell death of S. aureus.

aPDT-treated plasma using curcumin-poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles was investigated for COVID-19 therapy in order to overcome the poor bioavailability of curcumin due to its low water-solubility [20]. Curcumin could inactivate the SARS-CoV-2 without cytotoxicity on the Vero cells in the plasma. The experiments designed according to different concentrations of Cur-PLGANPs (3, 5, 7, and 10 % wt.), the irradiation times of blue laser (1, 3, and 5 min), and aPDT with the maximum dose of blue laser light (522.8 J/cm2) plus 10 % wt. Cur-PLGA-NPs had no cytotoxicity. Moreover, aPDT (10 % wt. Cur@PLGA-NPs in combination with blue laser light at 522.8 J/cm2) exhibited in vitro anti-COVID-19 activity without the cytotoxic effects in Vero cells culture by a treated plasma containing SARS−COV-2 (Fig. 2) [20].

There are several in vivo studies on the curcumin-mediated PDI. For example, Wu et al. [59] studied the effect of curcumin as a sensitizer for photodynamic therapy against murine norovirus 1 (MNV-1), a surrogate for NoV, in buffers and oysters. The data showed a dose-dependent change in viral morphology after photodynamic treatment with curcumin. This result was related to the destruction of viral nucleic acid integrity and viral capsid protein stability by the treatment. The evidence also suggests that photodynamic activation with curcumin is a potential, cost-effective approach to inactivate foodborne NoV when applied to oysters. All of this information suggests that aPDT with curcumin is a promising candidate for targeting SARS-CoV-2 virus [59]. However, much more needs to be done, especially in safety evaluation for human clinical trials. All the in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrate that aPDT with curcumin is a candidate to combat SARS-CoV-2. However, much more work needs to be done, especially in the safety assessment for human clinical trials.

Methylene blue (MB) or its derivatives such as dimethyl methylene blue (DMMB) are PSs with maximum absorption peaks of 670 and 648 nm, respectively [47]. MB has been utilized as an antidote to treat Methemoglobinemia and cyanide poisoning caused by nitrite. Thus, they represent a positive net charge, which can embed in DNA or RNA-based structures and negatively charged G-C base pairs of viral nucleic acids. During visible light exposure, the MB can absorb and excite energy, in parallel, during electron transfer, the ROS generation damages nucleic acids of viruses such as herpes viruses (HSV) and blocks DNA replication. Viral envelopes and nucleic acids can be considered as targets of attack. MB also can cause nucleic acid strand damage under light [47]. However, in the absence of light, the virucidal preventive or therapeutic activity of MB at low micromolar concentrations was reported against influenza virus H1N1 and SARS-CoV-2 [21,46,60].

MB ability to inhibit the protein-protein interaction of the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and its receptor ACE2, which is the first critical step initiating viral attachment and entry, could be a mechanism of action contributing to such activity. MB is a positively charged molecule, which can interact strongly with negative charged phosphate backbone of DNA or RNA, especially in combination with negatively charged G-C base pairs of viral nucleic acids and negatively charged envelope components [61,62]. The mechanism of interaction between cationic PSs with virus components has been described in section 2.1.4. Phthalocyanine (Pc) derivatives. Under visible light, MB generates singlet oxygen and other ROSs, which damages viral envelope and breaks nucleic acids to kill viruses. Additionally, MB-based PDT in combination with Radiochlorine photosensitizer may enhance SARS-CoV-2 inactivation, while inhibit virus replication within a monolayer of virus-sensitive Vero cells [21]. The study on combinatory MB-based PDI has been described in section 2.1.5.2. Combination of Ce6 with other photosensitizers.

Another exciting application of PDT is its potential ability to inactive viruses in blood and plasma, which has made MB an attractive anti-viral strategy in combination with convalescent plasma therapy. There are several in vitro studies and clinical trials using the MB-based PDT for virus inactivation in plasma, however no satisfactory performance has been achieved yet. In 1995, Bachmann et al. [63], reported that methylene and toluidine blue with low concentration can be useful for photo-inactivating HIV-1 viruses in plasma. using PCR assays, it was confirmed that the inactivation of HIV-1 could be resulted from its RNA destruction. In a retracted article, Jin et al. [64], claimed the inactivation of HIV and SARS-CoV-2 viruses using MB in plasma, proposing it as a safe treatment for AIDS and convalescent plasma therapy for COVID-19. In a randomized clinical trial, researchers assess whether early treatment of COVID-19 with high-titer MB-treated convalescent plasma. The results were disappointing, as MB-treated convalescent plasma did not decrease viral load in outpatients with COVID-19 and did not reduce hospitalization risk.

Several pathogen reduction technologies (PRT) have been approved and are currently marketed to minimize the residual risk of transfusion-transmitted infections associated with convalescent plasma therapy. With this aim, Eickmann et al. [13,65], designed THERAFLEX MB‐Plasma system to inactivate Ebola virus (EBOV and MERS‐CoV in platelet concentrate (PCs) and plasma, respectively [65]. The samples were exposed to visible light (MB/light; THERAFLEX MB-Plasma) and assessed for residual viral infectivity. MB/light decreased EBOV (≥4.6 log) and MERS‐CoV (≥3.3 log) titers in plasma to nondetectable levels. By extending this method, the potency of MB/light for the inactivation of pathogens was evaluated using the SARS-CoV, Nipah virus (NiV), Crimean–Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV), and other members of the Nairoviridae, Paramyxoviridae families. The results showed the plasma samples treated with MB and full light dose (120 J/cm2) diminished that of SARS-CoV (≥31 logs), CCHFV (≥32 logs) and NiV (≥27 log) to the LOD in plasma [13]. Similar to MB‐based PRT system, there is a marketed riboflavin-based system known as Mirasol PRT. A large clinical trial in Uganda has shown a safe and reliable application of riboflavin-based PRT for whole blood, that prevent seven targeted transfusion-transmitted infections, (malaria, bacteria, HIV, HBV, HCV, HEV, and herpesvirus) [41].

Another concern regarding the use of MB for PDI of plasma is its impact on the efficacy of convalescent plasma by altering the concentration and/or function of the neutralizing antibodies (nAbs). In a recent study, the impact of methylene blue (MB) plus visible light PRT was evaluated on the activity of nAbs against SARS-CoV-2. For this purpose, the neutralizing ability of anti-SARS-CoV-2 antibodies was tested before and after THERAFLEX MB-Plasma treatment. The results showed PRT with MB did not impair the convalescent plasma passive immunity preserving its potential therapeutic potency. This study concluded that PRT of COVID-19 convalescent plasma is a promising approach to diminish the risk for transmission of transfusion-associated infectious disease [66].

Some clinical trials have been performed to study MB efficacy, as a photosensitizer, in cancer treatment and the protection of serum from viruses. Likewise, a randomized, open-label clinical study was initiated on October 12, 2020. The COVID -19 outpatients were treated with MB and photodynamic therapy. For this purpose, the experimental group receives sublingual MB with low-level light therapy NocUlite. The primary outcome measures are changes in oxygen saturation measured with an oximeter (up to 7 days) and days to patient discharge (up to 7 days). Currently, the clinical trial is in the phase I recruiting stage (Clinical Trials. gov Identifier: NCT04619290) [11].

Regarding the effect of MB as an antidote in PDI for treating some types of microbial infections, some clinical studies have been conducted recently. For instance, Pires et al. [67] studied the effect of administering MB in the presence of 670 nm diode laser irradiation in positively diagnosed COVID-19 patients. The results of RT-qPCR experiment confirmed the variable effects on viral RNA molecules, which attributed to PDI-induced RNA cleavage causing falsely-elevated counts. In addition, 90% of samples from clinical nasal swabs showed reduced viral infectivity. This is the first case in which the efficacy of MB-mediated PDI as a front-line method to reduce local SARS-CoV-2 viral load has been demonstrated using quantitative clinical assessments of viral infectivity [67].

Indocyanine green (ICG) is a Food and Drug Administration–approved near-infrared fluorescent dye, employed as a PS agent for antimicrobial applications due to its attractive physicochemical properties, high sensitivity, and safety. ICG is an amphiphilic, water-soluble but relatively hydrophobic, anionic tricarbocyanine with a molecular weight of 776 Da. ICG is a near-infrared fluorescent contrast agent, as it emits NIR light around 830 nm after exposure to excitation lights between 750 and 800 nm [48,49]. ICG has very low toxicity, and binds to plasma protein almost momentarily and has a relatively short half-life into the bloodstream (3–5 min). Despite these advantages, the free ICG suffers from some drawbacks, such as relatively short circulation half-life, concentration-dependent aggregation, and rapid clearance from the body [48].

Herpes simplex virus (HSV1) often results in oral and perioral herpetic lesions. In a study by Namvar et al. [19], the influence of PDT with 810 and 940 nm diode laser and ICG photosensitizer on HSV-1 were investigated. They concluded HSV1 viruses in all samples were inactivated except in group ICG-without irradiation, signifying no dark toxicity. They also observed that 810 nm diode laser irradiation and ICG were more effective in HSV1 inactivation compared to 940 nm irradiation, maybe because of the peak absorption wavelength of ICG.

To control the spread of COVID-19, SARS-CoV-2-RBD was selected as a novel target for aPDT using ICG. The binding mode of the RBD to ICG was assessed via protein-ligand docking and MD simulation. RBD is a protein with a positive charge which could be a substrate for binding to negatively-charged ICG during aPDT. ICG can bind to the ligands of RBD such as NAG, GOL, and SO4 based on the results of docking and penetrate viral cells following light irradiation at a wavelength of 810 nm during aPDT. Generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) can collapse the structure of the viral cells and prevent viral attachment to the host cell surface [49].

One promising approach for utilizing ICG for targeted anti-viral therapy is its combination with Virus-like particles (VLPs), which have an internal hollow space and are often used as nano drug carriers [68]. Hepatitis B core protein virus-like particle (HBc VLP) is an impressive biomaterial, which has attracted considerable attention due to favorable properties such as structural stability, high uptake efficiency, and biocompatibility in biomedical applications [69]. It has been shown that ICG-loaded VLPs have good stability and biocompatibility, with desired targeting specificity and visualization of cancer cells [70], and potentially virus-infected cells.

Phthalocyanine (Pc) and their derivatives exhibit high extinction coefficients in near-infrared regions with excellent photodynamic and photo-thermal properties by means of structural regulation rather than formation of aggregates. Pc macrocycle has 16 positions where substitution may occur, significantly affecting its chemical properties. The planar macrocycle structure can be substituted for the non-peripheral (α) position and/or peripheral (β) positions. Depending on the metal center, the substituents can also be introduced into the axial positions [71]. When a functional group attaches at position 2, 9,16,23 or 2,3,9,10,16,17,23,24, it is termed as peripheral (β) position, while a functional group attaching at 1,8,15,22 or 1,4,8,11,15,18,22,25 position is termed as non-peripheral (α) position [72] (Fig. 2). Bulky periphery may influence the singlet oxygen generation efficacy of phthalocyanines. Tillo et al. [73], showed that an insertion of benzyloxy or 3,5-bis(benzyloxy)benzyloxy groups to the non-peripheral (α) positions of phthalocyanines improved the DMSO solubility, also the singlet oxygen generation efficacy compared with a phthalocyanine substituted with 2-propoxy groups.

As with porphyrins, metal atoms such as copper and zinc are usually inserted in the central ring of phthalocyanines. These metal-phthalocyanines are excited by UV and far-red light and emit far red fluorescence. The number of cationic charges and the way the cationic charges are distributed affect the phthalocyanine's amphiphilic character, which is an important property of antimicrobial PDI. Various metal-phthalocyanines with different charges have been studied for their capacity for photodynamic effects on viruses. Previous studies by Li et al. [74], indicated the cationic nanoemulsion of aluminum phthalocyanine has a higher antiviral activity against herpes simplex virus type 1 and vesicular stomatitis virus as compared to the counterpart anionic nanoemulsion. Different phthalocyanine derivatives (a mixture of sulfonated metal phthalocyanines) were examined for viral decontamination of blood manifesting a marked anti-herpesvirus effect [75]. In a designed model, the light source, by a cut-off filter (A> 605 nm), had an incident fluency rate of 200 W m−2. The ultra-structural examinations on the mode of the photosensitization capacity of phthalocyanine derivatives against HSV-1, HSV-2 and varicella zoster virus demonstrated a damage to the viral envelope which prevented viral adsorption and/or penetration. In conclusion, some amphiphilic Pc derivatives such as sulfonated Pc-naphthalocyanines AlNSB3P and AlN2SB2P appear to be effective sensitizers for PDI of free and intracellular enveloped viruses even 60 min after the onset of absorption. At the same time, the non-enveloped adenovirus was found to be resistant to all the dyes tested.

Despite several reports on the PDI against bloodborne viruses such as HIV-1 [8], the damage to red blood cells and platelets is still a limiting factor during viral PDI of blood. In a study, two synthesized water-soluble Zn (II) phthalocyanine (ZnPc) complexes with different cationic (ZnPcMe) and anionic (ZnPcS) charges, were used for photoinactivation of two DNA-containing enveloped viruses (HSV-1 and vaccinia virus), two RNA-containing enveloped viruses (bovine viral diarrhea virus and Newcastle disease virus) and two non-enveloped viruses (the enterovirus Coxsackie B1, an RNA-containing virus, and human adenovirus 5, a DNA virus). These two differently charged phthalocyanine complexes showed identically a marked virucidal effect against HSV-1 at an irradiation lasting 5 or 20 min (Δlog = 3.0 and 4.0, respectively). This virucidal effect was even lower for vaccinia virus, Δlog = 1.8 and 2.0 under the effect of ZnPcMe and ZnPcS, respectively. Bovine viral diarrhea virus manifested a moderate sensitivity to cationic ZnPcMe (Δlog = 1.8) and a pronounced one to anionic ZnPcS at 5- and 20-min irradiation (Δlog = 5.8 and 5.3, respectively). The complexes were unable to inactivate the Newcastle disease virus, Coxsackievirus B1 and human adenovirus type 5. The photo treatment data indicates that both singlet oxygen (type II reaction) and hydroxyl radical (type I reaction) play a role in viral inactivation. Furthermore, the data showed that lipid-enveloped viruses differ in their sensitivity to phthalocyanine photosensitization, as the non-enveloped viruses show more resistant [26].

To improve the phthalocyanine-based PDI against non-enveloped viruses, novel photosensitizing phthalocyanine derivatives have been designed and developed. A recent study shows a promising PDI potential of two phthalocyanine derivatives, tetra-methylpyridyloxy-substituted Ga (Ga4) and octa-methylpyridyloxy-substituted Ga (Ga8), on non-enveloped viruses, human adenovirus in particular. The results reveal no such effect was observed towards enveloped viruses, influenza A virus (IAV). In contrast, the PDI potential of Ga4 and Ga8 marked very high values on non-enveloped viruses, especially on human adenovirus (HAdV5), at which both the virucidal as well as the irradiation effects became combined [76].

Ultra-structural studies can reveal morphological differences between intact and photo-dynamically inactivated virions indicating inactivation mechanisms and molecular targets. Although all viral components can serve as potential molecular targets for ROS, the most readily available targets are proteins and unsaturated lipids of viral envelopes. Viral PDI using phthalocyanine derivatives can inactivate envelope viruses such as influenza and HIV-1 without total destruction of virion membranes. Using influenza as a model system, Korneev et al. [24], showed the irradiation with a relatively low concentration of the PS (cholinyl zinc phthalocyanine with 8 positive charges, Zn-PcChol8+) inactivated viral particles but did not destroy them. In this examination, by using a 30 W halogen lamp, the intensity of light reaching the probes was made up to 10 mW/cm2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that virion membranes kept structural integrity but lost their surface glycoproteins, forming “bald” virions. The increase of PS concentration from 2 μM to 4 μM leaded to destruction of lipid membranes. Thus, PDI of influenza virus initially results from surface protein removal, followed by complete virion destruction. This strategy using phthalocyanine has been applied to other enveloped viruses such as HIV-1 (strain X4 NL4-3) [77] and SARS-CoV-2 [27].

The negative electrostatic potential field of the viral envelope has a key role in virus interactions with cationic PSs (Fig. 3 ). Electrostatic map of the external surface of SARS-CoV-2 virion demonstrated the negatively charged envelope, which originated mainly from negatively charged lipid domains in the viral membrane and negatively charged areas on the “stalks” of the spike (S) proteins [78]. Further computational studies, conducted by Strakhovskaya group, showed the key interaction sites on Spike proteins of SARS-CoV, MERS-CoV, and SARS-CoV-2, which bind to PSs, octacationic octakis(cholinyl)zinc phthalocyanine (Zn-PcChol8+) and monocationic methylene blue (MB). Brownian Dynamics simulations revealed a major common PS binding site is at the connection of the S-protein stalk and head [62]. The lab experiments confirmed the MD simulation results. Briefly, Zn-PcChol8+ was studied in a minimal concentration of 1 μM, at light dose of 3.75 J/cm2 which the intensity reaching the probe made up 12.5 mW/cm2. The 5-min irradiation could completely neutralize the infectivity of SARS-CoV-2 when tested in Vero E6 cell cultures. This high sensitivity of SARS-CoV-2 to PDI by Zn-PcChol8+ emphasizes the potential application of this PS to control the COVID-19 infection (Fig. 2) [27]. Similar studies using electron microscopy showed morphological changes in X4 HIV-1 77, Bovine Coronavirus (BCoV) [61] and influenza H5N8 [24] viruses due to PDI by cationic PSs. The morphological changes are PS concentration-dependent, and include loss of spikes, change in shape, decreased size of virus particles, destruction of the envelope, agglomeration, and complete disintegration of viruses. In contrast, the virus PDI by anionic PSs include the photodamages limited to the surface glycoproteins. At the same time, membranes kept their structural integrity, as reported for PDI by anionic porphyrin [77] and PhytoQuin [79] against HIV-1 and human coronaviruses (HCoVs), respectively. Interestingly, PDI mediated by antibodies (so-called photoimmunotherapy, PIT) did not cause virion disintegration [77], as the physical damage in due to the antibody-envelope structural changes [80], not the PS-envelope interactions. According to literature, Pc may be a promising candidate as a photosensitizer in the treatment of some virus-associated cancers. For example, an in vivo experiment evaluated the efficacy of the phthalocyanine photosensitizer Pc 4 in the treatment of viral-induced (CRPV) papillomas. After papillomas developed, Pc4 was administered systemically, and 48 h later, samples were irradiated with 675 nm photoactivating light at either 100 or 150 J/cm2. According to the observations, the Pc-treated specimens exposed to higher light fluence (150 J/cm2) completely regressed and did not regrow within the observation period of up to 79 days. In addition, histological analysis confirmed the absence of residual tumor after complete response and replacement by near-normal epithelium [81]. However, it should be kept in mind that in this study PDT was used specifically for targeting tumors and not viruses.

Chlorin derivatives are excellent PSs for different treatments, including antimicrobial treatments. Chlorins (dihydroporphyrins) are a class of porphyrin macrocycles activated by light at 650 nm. Chlorin e6 (Ce6) is a second-generation PS, with some derivatives including Talaporfin®, Radachlorin® and Photoditazine® [83]. Photoditazine (PDZ) photosensitizer, a porphyrin derivative with a chlorine core, can potentially inactivate SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus [28]. Furthermore, there are some reports for the clinical application of 5-aminolaevulinic acid (ALA) mediated against diseases caused by viruses in humans.

One clinical study on 30 Mexican women has investigated the effectiveness of ALA-PDT in elimination of HPV-16 and HPV-18 associated with infection and/or cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN). Topical 6% 5-ALA in gel form was used to the uterine cervix; after 4 h, the lesion area was irradiated with a light dose of 200 J/cm2 at 635 nm. This clinical study has concluded that ALA-PDT is effective in eliminating HPV infection associated or not with CIN [84]. In another report of two cases in China, ALA-PDT has been proved as an effective and safe treatment for HPV induced anogenital condyloma acuminate [85].

The mechanism of Photoditazine-mediated PDI compared to the UVC inactivation using a viral-cell model was investigated further. Photoditazine (PDZ) is an N-methyl-d-glucosamine derivative of chlorine e6 that is water soluble and has an intense absorption in the range of 650–680 nm [86]. Accordingly, a serial dilution of 10, 50 and 250 μg/mL PDZ was mixed with the pseudoviruses and irradiated using a LED at 670 nm. All irradiations were performed with an irradiance rate of 30 mW/cm2 in a time-dependent manner of 1, 10 and 20 min, equaling the light doses of 1.8, 18 and 36 J/cm2, respectively. In parallel model, UVC at 254 nm was delivered by 1, 6 and 36 s corresponding to doses of 10, 60 and 360 mJ/cm2, respectively. The quantitative PCR results showed that dose-dependent manner of UVC irradiation mostly damages ssRNA while PDZ-based PDI mostly destroy the spike and viral membrane, in concentration and dose-dependent manners. In conclusion, the ACE2-expressing cells infected by virus and treated with either UVC or PDZ-based PDI resulted in complete viral inactivation despite the presence of RNA and DNA intact genes [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combination of Ce6 with other photosensitizers
ce:section.ce:para: Combining Ce6 with other photosensitizers with similar attributes may have a synergic effect for PDI. In a comparative in vitro study, combining Ce6 derivative Radachlorin with methylene blue (MB) resulted in higher PDI against SARS-CoV-2 virion and virus-infected cells at 662 nm in doses of 16 J/cm2 and 40 J/cm2 laser irradiation. Radachlorin, also known in the EU as Bremachlorin, is a composition of chlorophyll derivatives in an aqueous solution. The antiviral activity of PDT accompanied by MB and Radachlorin manifested as the direct viral inactivation in biological fluids, while protecting Vero cells infected by SARS-CoV-2 from viral lysis. The absence of viral lesions in the cellular monolayers suggests that the combinatory PDT completely inhibited viral replication in the sensitive cells. A 100–1000 times lower concentration of this combinatory PDI has shown to bear better medicinal effectiveness, widely applied in Russia [21]. Interestingly, the anti-viral activity of MB and Radachlorin was observed even 3.5 h after cell infection with IC50 of 0.66 and 2.0 μg/mL, respectively. This observation indicates that PSs could potentially be used for the successful treatment of coronavirus infection, however, further studies are needed to clarify the mechanism of anti-viral activity within the infected cells.

The antiviral activity of Ce6 derivative Radachlorin is supposedly similar to the MB which is based on several mechanisms of action including genomic RNA degradation [60]. Besides, Radachlorin showed significant antiviral activity in the absence of light irradiation [21], that is similar to previous reports on the dark toxicity of MB against SARS-CoV-2 isolated from a clinical specimen [60]. In conclusion, these data open the possibility to investigate the combinatory effect of Radachlorin and MB, as a potential therapeutic agent against SARS-CoV-2 in clinical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the anti-viral activity of Ce6 with phthalocyanine-based compounds
ce:section.ce:para: In a comparative study using a SARS-CoV-2 pseudotyped model, the anti-viral activity of Ce6 was compared with zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc5K), demonstrating both PSs were promising candidates for SARS-CoV-2 inactivation while maintaining no toxicity to cells. The inactivation of SARS-CoV-2 in the forms of live virus and pseudovirus, either outside or within the host cells without significant cytotoxicity using compounds Ce6 and zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc5K) was reported [25]. Surprisingly, the ZnPc5K had more potency with an EC50 of 60.2 nM, much higher than the typical micromolar range potencies for antimicrobial or antitumor applications of PSs.

Besides the advantages, there are limitations for use of the chlorin derivatives. Due to the short half-life (<40 ns) of 1O2 during PDT, each 1O2 molecule can only have therapeutic effects in the area of diameter less than 20 nm [87]. Moreover, nonspecific activation of PSs will cause potential cytotoxicity in normal tissue. Thus, selected delivery of PSs to target is high desired. Beyond the previously mentioned challenges, the vast majority of PSs, including Ce6, have a poor water solubility, which leads to undesired pharmacokinetics [88]. In a clinical trial, meso-tetra-(hydroxyphenyl)-chlorin (m-THPC) was used as a photosensitizer in a PDT approach for the treatment of recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. In this study, 23 patients were administered m-THPC intravenously and then directly exposed to endoscopic PDT with 80–100 J of light in adults and 60–80 J in children. The results illustrated that the immune response to the virus improved with clinical response, but there was no change in the prevalence of latent human papillomavirus DNA. In conclusion, m- THPC PDT was found to reduce the severity of laryngeal papillomas, possibly by improving the immune response [89].

Riboflavin (or vitamin B2) and its derivatives, flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) have key role in providing cellular metabolism. Flavins are also involved in the cell signaling or gene expression due to strong UV-blue light absorption, which results in the generation of highly oxidising excited states that can elicit biological signaling events or responses [90].

Besides, photon-induced excitation of flavins in the UV-blue spectral band makes them efficient photosensitizers inducing oxidative damage to light exposed-organism. Flavins, as anti-microbial PSs, may inactivate the virus through formation of either singlet oxygen (1O2) via energy transfer to environmental oxygen (Type II), or hydrogen peroxide and derivatives via radicalisation (Type I), as described in section 2. Riboflavin has been approved for anti-viral PDI in plasma and platelets, due to its excellent photosensitizing properties, abundance and lack of toxicity. More recently, it was shown to inactivate coronaviruses such as MERS and SARS-CoV-2 viruses [91].

Designing of flavin derivatives for virus inactivation depends on the virion structure as well as the mechanism of PDI [92]. The prepared flavins demonstrate high efficacy and rapid inactivation of mouse hepatitis virus strain A59 (MHV-A59), as a surrogate model of SARS-CoV-2. By applying white light irradiation (400–700 nm), it has been shown that guanidinylated flavin produced the greatest viral PDI, with a titer decrease of >4 log10 after 10 min of irradiation (21.0 J/cm2 light dose) in the presence of 5 μM compound. This observation depicts the structure–function relationship correlating to the functionality of flavins derivatives. Furthermore, the comparison between anti-viral PDI and anti-bacterial PDI using flavin derivatives revealed functional group dependency for each pathogen [92].

In a clinical trial, riboflavin was used as a photosensitizer for PDT in 20 COVID-19 patients. Systemic administration of riboflavin was followed by local treatment of the respiratory tract with special applicators for the nose and mouth/throat. The adapter for the nose contains 1 blue LED diode (447 nm) and 1 UVA LED diode (375 nm), and the applicator for the mouth/throat contains 14 blue LED diodes (447 nm) and 14 UVA LED (375 nm). After 5 days of PDT treatment, all 20 patients in the experimental group showed significant improvement in clinical symptoms and viral load assessment. In addition, it was observed that after this period, 14 of the 20 patients had a negative qPCR test, while the other 6 patients also had a significantly reduced viral load [93].

Recently, many researchers have studied the application of photo-activated carbon nanomaterials (CNM) such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene oxides for microbial detection and destruction [94–98]. CNMs have some exceptional features that make these materials promising agents for PDT and photothermal treatment (PTTs). CNMs may increase ability to absorb light and maintain their chemical and water stability. Furthermore, they have high anti-viral activity, high bacterial toxicity with negligible mammalian cytotoxicity [94–98].

It has been shown that a population of the singlet excited state 1C60* can be generated by the excitation of C60 ground state (1C60) using UV or visible light. The stability of this middle state is almost short (<1.3 ns). Besides lowering energy by internal conversion or fluorescence emission, the 1C60* experiences fast intersystem crossing (ISC) to the long-standing excited triplet-state 3C60*. The yield of triplet-state is close to a unit, and the lifespan is about 50–100 μs. Under aerobic conditions, ROS can be generated by interacting 3C60 with ground-state molecular oxygen (O2 (3Σ-g)). This activity occurs by transferring electron or energy to generate superoxide anion radical (O2 .-) or singlet molecular oxygen, 1O2 (1Δg), respectively. Fullerenes are exceptionally acceptable 1O2 (1Δg) generators with a quantum yield that is near unity. Therefore, 3C60* or C60 .- can form (O2 .-) by shifting an electron to molecular oxygen. In contrast to the generation of 1O2 (1Δg), the electron transfer preferentially can happen in polar solvents, especially in attendance of reducing agents such as NADH. The processes of 1O2(1Δg) and (O2 .-) generation are equivalent to the particular photochemical reaction types recognized as type II and type I photochemical mechanisms, respectively [99]. The type II and type I mechanisms are described in section 2.

Fullerene can produce singlet oxygen with a high quantum yield illuminated in the visible region (535 nm) [100]. The singlet oxygen has been well employed for microbial inactivation and the removal of pollutants [101]. Singlet oxygen act as an oxidant and has a longer lifetime (s), 2–4μs compared with hydroxyl radicals (1 ns). They are also more specific with a low reduction potential, while hydroxyl radicals are non-selective. There are several effective approaches for the synthesis of Fullerenes and their derivatives in the forms of either water-soluble or insoluble, and testing them against viruses such as HSV-1 [102,103], HIV-1 [104], SARS-CoV-2 [105], cytomegalovirus (HCMV) [103], Semliki Forest virus (SFV, Togaviridae) [106], vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV, Rhabdoviridae) [106], Influenza A virus (IAV) [107], and the MS2 bacteriophage [17].

Four different photosensitized aqueous fullerene suspensions aqu-nC60, C60(OH)6, C60(OH)24, C60(NH2)6 were used for the inactivation of the MS2 bacteriophage [17]. Two 15 W fluorescent bulbs provided low-pressure UV irradiation, which had an output spectrum peak at 365 nm in the UVA region and an intensity of 2.3 mW/cm2. UVA-sensitized C60(NH2)6 generated 1O2 at the highest rate (0.969 ± 0.039 μM min−1) followed by C60(OH)24 (0.686 ± 0.011 μM min−1), C60(OH)6 (0.563 ± 0.014 μM min−1), and aqu-nC60 (0.132 ± 0.004 μM min−1). Singlet oxygen generation depends on the quantum yield of primary nanoparticles and aggregate morphology. Consequently, aqu-nC60, C60(OH)6, C60(OH)24, and C60(NH2)6 increased MS2 inactivation rates by a factor of 1.7, 5.1, 5.5, and 11.4 respectively compared with UVA alone following the same trend as 1O2 production. They showed the singlet oxygen generation can change the secondary structures of capsid protein and protein oxidation. 3-amino-propyl trietoxy silane can be successfully exploited for the functionalization of hot-pressed silica particles on stainless-steel mesh. In this case, the amine groups of 3-amino-propyltrietoxysilane to the double bonds of C60 led to the production of recyclable sensitizers. C60 immobilized on the amine-functionalized silica-coated stainless-steel mesh, then had been activated by visible-light to generate singlet oxygen. The MS2 bacteriophage virus inactivation was effectively observed around 10–15 cm from the surface. Viral disinfection efficiencies at a distance of 5 cm from the light source steadily increased with an increasing photo-illumination time; irradiating C60/SiO2–NH2 SS with visible light for 0.5, 1, and 3 h resulted in the inactivation efficiencies of MS-phage of 20.3, 24.6, and 52.6%, respectively [108].

Solid-phase fullerene-based photosensitizer could be developed towards anti-viral PDI in protein plasma preparations of donor blood. Belousova et al. [107], studied the ability of the solid-phase fullerene to inactivate the enveloped influenza virus in protein fraction of donor blood. In optimized conditions, a complete inactivation of enveloped virus of extremely high initial titer (7.0–9.5 log10EID 50/0.2 mL) in the solution of albumin was achieved after a short time as 30 min of irradiation. This process did not affect the oxidative metabolism of neutrophils and membranes of erythrocytes. The results suggested that the method can be recommended for further development and optimization of virus inactivation in the preparations of the donor blood plasma.

Nanocomposite films based on conjugates of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWNT) and protoporphyrin IX (PPIX) are highly effective against bacteria [109] and virus [110] upon irradiation with visible light. Banerjee et al. [110], synthesized porphyrin-conjugated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (NT-P) as potent antiviral agents, attaching PPIX to acid-functionalized MWNTs. The unconjugated PPIX had an intense peak at 404 nm, while after conjugation with MWNT, a slight red shift of the peaks (from 404 to 409 nm) was observed. In the presence of 30-min visible light irradiation (350 W), NT-P significantly reduced the ability of Influenza A virus to infect mammalian cells. NT-P conjugates represent a new approach for preparing ex vivo reusable antiviral agents. This finding could be practical for applying other MWNTs for virus inactivation.

Carbon dots are a few nanometers in size semiconductors, having optical and electronic properties that differ from larger particles due to quantum mechanics. When the quantum dots are illuminated by UV light, an electron in the quantum dot can be excited to a state of higher energy [111]. Carbon dots have proved promising application against different types of corona viruses. They could bind directly to viral surface proteins and stop the first step of viral attachment to the host cells [112,113]. On the other hand, Carbon dots have many advantages over conventional organic PSs, such as chemical inertness, high water solubility, photo-stability, interplay between optoelectronic features and shape and size, good donors in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer process, high stability in physiological conditions, specific accumulation at the target site and facile surface functionalization [114]. These features therefore make carbon dots promising candidates in novel delivery systems for target-specific photosensitization due to their photoluminescence properties [115].

CDots may represent a new class of antiviral drugs that can be used to prevent and control viral infections or spread viruses such as SARS-CoV-2 as the molecular aspects become better understood. In an investigation [116], the CDots containing 2,20-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) (EDA) were chosen for antiviral evaluation of carbon dots (CDots) with visible light. It was decided to use the bacteriophage MS2 model as a surrogate for small RNA viruses. The results highlighted that the CDots mediated PDI was concentration-dependent, as the higher concentration of EDA-CDots in the visible light treatment, the more dramatically the viral titer decreased. In addition, the MS2 phages in the samples were inactivated entirely after 24 h of treatment with 20 mg mL−1 EDA-CDots (>8 log decrease in viral titer), or > 6 log reduction and about 1 log reduction (90% reduction in virus) after 16 h and 2 h of treatment, respectively. The results indicated the MS2 phage photoinactivation by EDA CDots was time-dependent. Moreover, the observations clearly demonstrate that CDots triggered by visible light are highly efficient antiviral agents that reduce the ability of bacteriophage MS2 to infect host cells. The integrity and shape of the MS2 phage did not appear to be significantly altered by the action of the photoexcited CDots, nor were the capsid proteins degraded. However, protein carbonylation-a common marker of protein oxidation-and genomic RNA degradation did occur. The mechanism underlying the antiviral effect and outcome is similar to PDI, which is more commonly known for activating the antibacterial function of CDots in response to visible/natural light [116]. The ROS generation from CDots could potentially allow controlled therapeutic dosing; therefore, implementing the PDT technique within carbon dots can produce more effective results against pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2.

Considering the pros and cons of CDots mediated PDI, carbon structures have advantages such as chemical inertness, biocompatibility, large surface area for absorbing light and molecules, which enable CDots to have various anti-viral applications as photodynamic biomaterials, antimicrobial materials, and light-responsive drug delivery against viral infection. In contrast, some disadvantages such as poor solubility in physiological environment, limited photodynamic activity, limited size and shape control have led researchers to further research on such materials [117].

Titanium oxide (TiO2) is able to mediate photo-oxidation and could generate ROS on light absorption by electron transfer reactions involving oxygen and water. According to studies, TiO2 can generate ROS through electron transfer reactions at the presence of oxygen and water. One of the draw-back of TiO2-nanoparticles application for medical purposes is their fundamental absorption in the UV region. Hence, scientists have considered shifting the absorbance spectrum of these nanoparticles in the direction of visible region through doping with materials such as platinum or nitrogen [118–120]. Several articles in the field of omitting viruses from air and water employing antiviral PDT [121,122], as well as introducing self-sterilizing materials and surfaces stand on photosensitizer-loading published [7,123,124]. Notably, some inorganic materials have been evaluated for this purpose [125]. Using environmentally friendly and cheap inorganic materials alongside sunlight as a natural light source represents a considerable offer for this application of aPDT. One example of such inorganic materials is TiO2, which in the presence of sunlight can induce photo-catalysis for aPDT of viruses [126–128].

Photo-catalytic disinfection of murine norovirus 1 (MNV-1, a surrogate for HuNoV) was significantly higher under UVC with TiO2, compared with UVB and UVA. The efficiency and mechanism of MNV-1 inactivation was studied on an experimentally prepared solidified agar matrix (SAM) to simulate blueberries using different wavelengths (A, B, C) of UV light, both with and without TiO2 photo-catalysis (TP). MNV-1 was inoculated on exterior and interior of SAM and inactivation efficiencies of different treatments were investigated using several assays. Initial inoculum levels of MNV-1 on the SAM surface and interior were 5.2 log PFU/mL. UVC with TiO2 (UVC-TP) achieved the highest level of viral reduction for both externally inoculated and internalized MNV-1. Externally inoculated MNV-1 was reduced to non-detectable levels after UVC-TP treatment for 5 min while there was still a 0.9 log viral titer after UVC alone. For internalized MNV-1, 3.2 log and 2.7 log reductions were obtained with UVC-TP and UVC alone treatments for 10 min, respectively [129]. The biocidal action of TiO2 photo-catalysis has been ascribed to major photochemical oxidants, such as OH radicals. UVC induced more production of OH radicals during photo-catalysis than UVB and UVA. An excellent correlation was found between [•OH] ss and viral inactivation. Their study demonstrated the efficacy of UVC-TP against MNV-1 on an experimental scale. Inactivation of Human Coronavirus has been shown by titania nanoparticle coatings (300 μg/mL TiO2 nanoparticle, UVC light 254 nm, 10 min) and UVC light 254 nm, 10 min. The results prove the virucidal efficacy of photoactive TNPs deposited on glass coverslips (Fig. 2) [16]. Further studies will cover the feasibility for scale-up of this disinfection technology to pilot and commercial scales and use against other pathogenic microorganisms such as SARS-CoV-2 129.

Similar to carbon structures, the TiO2 nanosheets have large surface area as well as adjustable band gap for efficient photoactivity. The biocompatibility of the TiO2 [130] and Titanium oxynitride (TiOxNy) [131] thin films was analyzed using primary cultures indicated that they were appropriated for culture of living cells which displayed normal electrical behavior. Hence, TiO2 can be used as an actuator, photocatalyst and antimicrobial material in PDI. Despite these potential applications, more studies needed for the unknown toxicity of degradation products of TiO2 films. Other limitations of TiO2 include its weak photoactivity to long-wavelength light, and limited surface functionalization strategies [132].

ce:section.ce:section-title: New generation of photosensitizers and their derivatives for viral PDI
ce:section.ce:para: With an approach to improving PS-based PDI, there are two interests in PS development towards desired pharmaceutical properties and clinical applications. One ongoing interest is the improvement of ‘classical or old-style’ PSs via combination with other compartments. The other growing interest is discovering and synthesizing novel PS derivatives through modification and optimization of classical PSs [133]. With this approach, the second-generation PSs have mainly been derived from tetrapyrrole family members, particularly porphyrins and porphyrin-related structures. Tetrapyrrole-based compounds involve ‘classical’ PSs, such as benzoporphyrins, chlorins, and phthalocyanines [134], which have been widely applied for anti-viral PDI, as discussed in this review. These PSs are also known as second-generation PSs, which were developed in an attempt to overcome the disadvantages of first-generation compounds.

One of the new compound classes investigated for their potential in the PDI of viruses is a new aryl methyldiene rhodanine derivative, LJ001, and oxazolidine-2,4-dithione, JL103, that act on the viral lipid membrane, inhibiting its fusion with a target cell membrane [135]. The anti-viral mechanism of the rhodanine derivatives is based on photooxidation of virus envelope lipids, which make them ideal antivirals with broad-spectrum.

Another promising class of PSs suitable for anti-viral PDI includes the synthetic dyes such as phenothiazinium, squaraine and distyryl boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes [136]. The BODIPY scaffold has great potential as a source of antiviral agents, due to its photophysical properties and the ability to generate singlet oxygen. The BODIPY-based PSs are equipped with heavy halogen atoms, such as Br and I, in the organic chromophore to make compounds reach the triplet excited state by quenching the fluorescence and facilitating intersystem crossing. In one study, DIMPy-BODIPY could reduce the viral infectivity by 6 log units in dengue-1 virus (0.1 μM), by 5 log units (0.5 μM) in vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV), and by 2 log units (5 μM) in human adenovirus-5 (HAd-5). In another study, BODIPY-embedded electrospun materials showed a complete inactivation of VSV [137], even at low PS loadings. Impressively, detection limit inactivation (67 PFU mL−1) of VSV was observed (99.99%, 4 log units; P = 0.0058) after 1 h irradiation with 65 ± 5 mW cm−2, 400–700 nm. However, due to incidental light exposure, non-photodynamic (dark) inactivation was also observed. Recently, non-photodynamic antiviral potential has been shown in the methylene blue [21], tetrapyrrole Radachlorin [21], and derivatives of anionic [138] and cationic [61] phthalocyanine.

For new generation of PSs, scientists seek new designs with shorter periods of photosensitization, higher singlet oxygen yield, and longer excitation wavelengths to activate the PS in deeper tissue. The latter could be improved with new strategies of activation via combination with upconverting nanomaterials, as discussed in section 2.4.2. Regarding the clinical application, the improved new PSs should have a reduced toxicity to healthy tissues and a lower incidence of side effects.

Photosensitizers with Aggregation-Induced emission characteristics (AIEgens) are introduced with their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, and moldability [139]. Membrane-targeting PSs are a class of new PSs with AIEgens which may give capabilities in both prominent fluorescence imaging behavior and extraordinary ROS generation to inactivate the pathogens. Recently, the efficient PDI of SARS-CoV-2 virus has been reported by employing PSs with aggregation‐induced emission characteristics (DTTPB) [50]. This molecule structure has a hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails, mimicking the structure of phospholipids on biological membranes (Fig. 2). The fascinating attributes making DTTPB an excellent candidate for anti-viral PDI include high molar absorbance coefficient, broad absorption band covering the whole white light region, elevated ROS sensitizing efficiency, specific membrane targeting and binding to the virus envelope. Employing membrane‐targeting AIE‐type PS for efficient PDI is expected to inspire new preventive strategies for photo-controlled photodynamic killing of SARS-CoV-2 and other pathogens [50,140–142].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combination of photosensitizer-mediated PDI with photobiomodulation (PBM)
ce:section.ce:para: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT), as a PBM method, is a photon therapy based on the use of a light source such as lasers or light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with a particular wavelength of visible or near-infrared light [6,143,144]. Studies showed that intravenous lasers with different wavelengths could be used for oxygenation (green), viral reduction and increasing NO (blue), and increasing ATP (red).

For the treatment of Recurrent Herpes Labialis (RHL) caused by HSV-1, high-intensity laser therapy (HILT) following LLLT was applied alone [23], or in combination with methylene blue [145]. In one study without applying PDT [23], the Er,Cr:YSGG laser device was used as a HILT with a wavelength of 2.78 μm, a pulse duration of 140–200 μs, a fixed repetition rate of 20 Hz, and output power of 0.75 W, so that pain could be reduced during irradiation. This study was extended towards combination with PDT method using the methylene blue (MB) at a concentration of 0.01% (m/V) [145]. Twenty-four hours after HILT and the MB-mediated PDT, the LLLT method was performed using a diode laser with a continuous red-light emission wavelength of 660 nm. The energy density was 3.8 J/cm2 per point, with an output power of 15 mW and 0.15 J of energy per point. LLLT was performed 24, 48, and 72 h and 7 days after HILT and PDT. The results demonstrated the efficacy of HILT or MB-mediated PDT in combination with LLLT in the treatment of herpes labialis. HILT can reduce the amount of virus present in the fluid and can be used to promote rupture and empty the vesicles. On the other hand, PDT seems to be an effective therapeutic option that does not have the problem of heating that occurs with high-intensity laser techniques. In addition, PDT is a more cost-effective technique compared with HILT. Therefore, MB-mediated PDT combined with LLLT may be an appropriate treatment for RHL viral infection [145].

To determine the effect of USP laser on the inactivation of MNV (Murine Norovirus), a tunable mode-locked Ti-Sapphire laser was employed at 425 nm [146] These pulses were irradiated a doubled frequency to make fs pulses at wavelengths 400, 408, 425, 450, 465, and 510 nm with an average power of 150 mW. In this study, the sensitivity of MNV to singlet oxygen was evaluated by using singlet oxygen enhancers (Rose bengal and riboflavin and methylene blue at a final concentration of 0.1%). The effects of viral inactivation compared to untreated controls were assessed using oxygen quenchers. The results suggested that the mechanism of the laser-induced inactivation of viruses is independent of wavelength. Furthermore, they used continuous wavelength (CW) (408 nm) and observed this type of laser could significantly inactivate MNV during irradiation. Using photosensitizers, riboflavin, rose bengal and methylene blue that generate singlet oxygen substantially improves the inactivation efficiency. The result also indicated that using this technique and virus inactivation does not necessarily require USP.

The classical PSs lack sufficient selectivity and are taken up in the neighboring normal (or uninfected) cells, resulting in undesirable adverse effects. To overcome this obstacle, the third-generation PSs have been modified from classical PSs, which have been weaponized with targeting tools for specific killing of virus-infected cells. Targeted PDT uses PSs conjugated with viral-targeting tools, such as antibodies, peptides, and other ligands with specific receptors or encapsulated into carriers (such as liposomes, micelles, nanoparticles). These tools may specifically target cell-free viruses (known as virions) or viral particles within the infected cells, such as viral Env and spike. The targeted viral particles could be from either original virus during the cell internalization or the viral particles expressing by infected cell during virus replication. Advances in immunotherapy and nanotechnology have significantly improved antimicrobial PDI, giving rise to approaches such as photoimmunotherapy (PIT), nanoparticle delivery, and the use of upconverting nanoparticles to activate the PSs in deep tissue.

When a PS is conjugated with an antibody, the ROS generation can physically damage the antibody-receptor complex leading to viral inactivation. As a model for HIV immunotherapy, Sadraeian et al. targeted HIV Env expressing cells by HIV antibody conjugated with cationic Porphyrin or anionic phthalocyanine PS. They showed when photo-immunoconjugates (PICs) bound to HIV Env on the cell membrane, the physical changes in the structure of irradiated PS-antibody may physically damage the membrane resulting in water penetration and rapid necrotic cell death. This mechanism is independent of the PS type [147] (Fig. 4 A). The second study demonstrated how PICs can inactivate the HIV virion as a model for viral photo-inactivation. In this model, PIC binds gp41 at the site of interaction with gp120, the singlet oxygen generation causes physical changes in PIC-Env complex and makes the HIV virion unable to infect the CD4+ T lymphocyte cell [8] (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PDI in deep tissue using up conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: Most powerful PSs are optimally excited with UV or short wavelength (blue) visible light; however, these wavelengths possess inadequate tissue penetration ability [148]. Near-infrared (NIR) light with the wavelength of 700–1000 nm has been considered to be a biological transparency window that exhibits greater tissue penetration depth and minimal photo-damage to tissue, because such light has less absorption and less scattering within biological tissue [149]. UCNPs are composed of rare-earth based lanthanide- or actinide-doped transition metals such as sodium yttrium fluoride (NaYF4) co-doped with ytterbium (Yb3+) and erbium (Er3+), which can convert near-infrared (NIR) light into visible or even ultraviolet light [150]. Importantly, UCNPs can efficiently convert the deeply penetrating NIR light to visible wavelengths that allow for the activation of nearby PS agents to produce singlet oxygen [151,152]. Therefore, recent studies have proposed that UCNPs could offer potentially valuable benefits for PDI in deep tissue attributed to their distinct photon upconverting feature (Fig. 5 ).

Conjugation of UCNP-PSs with targeting tools such as antibodies could improve the selective killing of not only virions but also the virus-infected cells. Sadraeian et al. [153], reported a NIR-PDT strategy targeting both HIV Env-expressing cells and free-cell HIV virus via conjugating the UCNP with antibody-PSs. This complex nanoparticle has three compartments; Anti-HIV antibody for targeting HIV Env, UCNP for photon or energy source in deep tissue, and porphyrin PS for 1O2 generation [153]. Furthermore, Lanthanide-doped UCNP could be used to label viral envelope and nucleic acid, for in vitro virus tracking inside the cell and also in vivo monitoring of viral infection [154].

In another study, Lim et al. presented a UCNP-based PDI strategy that effectively reduced the infectious virus titers in vitro and in the murine model and increased target specificity to virus-infected cells. These nanoparticles were practical in reducing dengue virus serotype 2 (DENV2) titers and adenovirus type 5 (Ad5V) [155]. In addition, up-converted zinc phthalocyanine nanoparticles were reported to bring about >5 log PFU/mL reduction of the dengue virus with 980 nm light through the production of singlet oxygen [155]. In a recent study on utilizing UCNP against COVID-19, Kumar et al. [156], developed a Yttrium Orthosilicate-based UCNP coated on Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) surfaces and cationic and anionic polymers. An oligosaccharide species, chosen to possess strong bond affinity/complementarity to SARS-CoV-2 Spike glycoprotein, was grafted to the polymer to add one more mechanism of virus inactivation. The results showed that two formulations, Y2SiO5(1% Pr, 1% Gd) and Y2SiO5 (1.2% Pr, 1.2% Gd, 7.2% Li) had reduced active coronavirus viral load 4*104 to 0 in an hour of exposure to white light [156].

Although Lanthanide-doped UCNPs have shown high upconversion efficiency, high resistance to photobleaching, and narrow emission bandwidth, some limitations of applying them for PDI include the uncertain toxicity of degradation products, and limited functionalization strategies [117], which demands more studies in this field.

In this article, we tried to review the processes of virus inactivation by irradiation methods with the help of photosensitizers. To solve this pandemic, these materials-based techniques can be effective and applicable solutions and alternatives for eliminating fatal viruses from human life. Since each photosensitizer has its own advantages and disadvantages, deciphering the mechanism of viral inactivation may help us improve each method's efficacy. The irradiation strategies which damage viral envelope proteins (e.x. photoimmunotherapy) have advantages over the other strategies destroying only nucleic acids (e.x. UVC method), as they are resistant to virus mutations, whereas they could be ineffective against non-enveloped viruses. In choosing the appropriate radiation method and material, we should consider material characterizations and radiation type and parameters, and the size, shape, and type of virus. Application of PSs with no targeting tools may damage healthy cells instead of infected cells or virions, and inevitably lead to unwanted side effects. To solve this issue, there are recent developments and applications in delivery tools, such as targeting antibodies and nanoparticles, which could be combined with PSs for improved specific utility.

In conclusion, such an anti-viral PDI strategy is implementable and has unique advantages, including independence of the viral antigen mutations as the viruses cannot become resistant to singlet oxygen and other ROSs, repeatability, and affordability compared to the monoclonal antibodies, and lack of long-term toxicity of materials. However, the traditional PSs have several major limitations in the clinics, which demand the rational design of novel new PSs to optimize the pharmaceutical properties, such as poor chemical purity, low attenuation coefficients, the long half-life, and excessive accumulation into the target.

The world is confronting the COVID-19 pandemic, which is causing numerous deaths in all countries. With the growing challenge of COVID-19, several researches have been performed to overcome this pandemic. Therapies based on physical waves and PDI only or in combination with materials may be practical for inactivating viruses. Accordingly, using these methods alongside applying biomaterials and nanomaterials may lead to a new generation of PSs to inactivate viruses. Natural or synthetic biomaterials can be immobilized with specific sensitizers to effectively obtain antiviral features in the presence of irradiation at a particular wavelength. The UCNP linked with markers can also be efficient in targeting and trapping viruses via NIR radiation which is penetrable in the deep tissue. To increase the specificity of the conventional PDT techniques that lead to parallel damage to healthy cells, UCNPs can be surface modified for bio-conjugation targeting ligands and antibodies.

Future directions to improve PDI performance include genetically encoded protein PSs, photochemical internalization, two-photon absorption PDT, and enhancing ROS generation. The most common working mechanism of PDT/PDI is based on high-toxicity singlet oxygen, which is called Type II PDT. But it is highly dependent on oxygen consumption. A recent study reports biotinylation of Type II PSs may enhance or trigger the generation of anion radicals, which alleviates the limitation of traditional photodynamic [157].

Furthermore, combinations of PDI with other treatments, such as chemotherapy, sonodynamic therapy, and immunotherapy, may be a promising approach for selectively killing the viral infection. Besides, simple strategies, such as in combination with simple inorganic salts, may enhance the phototoxicity of PSs.

J., FL210100180).
M.S., F.A, E.B. and D.J. conceptualized and designed the project, and wrote the main manuscript text. M.S. and L.Z. prepared figures. All authors reviewed and edited the manuscript.
